title: Advancing Shark and Ray Conservation: Indonesia’s Voice at Sharks International 2026

content.text: Sharks International is the world’s largest scientific conference dedicated to sharks, rays, and chimaeras, bringing together researchers, conservation practitioners, policymakers, and fisheries experts from around the world every four years. At the 2026 conference, held in Colombo, Sri Lanka (4–8 May), the Illegal Wildlife Trade Challenge Fund (IWTEX005) Catch Aware project played a key role in amplifying Indonesian voices, supporting strong representation from national researchers, practitioners, and policymakers in global discussions on shark and ray conservation.

The 2026 conference marked a historic milestone as it was held in Asia for the first time. More than 800 participants from over 80 countries gathered at the Sirimavo Bandaranaike Memorial Exhibition Centre to exchange knowledge, discuss emerging challenges, and explore solutions for the conservation and sustainable management of sharks and rays. For Indonesia, one of the world’s largest shark and ray fishing nations, the conference provided an opportunity not only to share experiences from the field, but also to contribute to global conversations on fisheries management, conservation, trade governance, and the implementation of international commitments under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES).

For the first time, Indonesia ranked among the top five most represented countries at Sharks International, alongside Australia, the USA, and the UK. With 46 abstracts accepted (7% of the total), Indonesia had a significant presence at the conference, reflecting both its exceptional shark and ray biodiversity and its central role in global shark fisheries and trade.

Members of the Illegal Wildlife Trade Challenge Fund (IWTEX005) Catch Aware team from Rekam Nusantara Foundation, Cefas, Indonesia's Ministry of Marine Affairs and Fisheries (MMAF), IPB University, and the National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN) attended the conference and delivered nine scientific presentations, alongside hosting and participating in an international expert panel. Together, these contributions showcased how science, community engagement, digital innovation, and policy development can work together to support shark and ray conservation in Indonesia.
Some of the main themes of the conference included:
Building Better Data for Shark and Ray Management

A recurring theme across Indonesia's contributions was the importance of reliable data. Effective conservation and fisheries management depend on understanding what species are being caught, where they are landed, how populations are changing over time, and how products move through supply chains. Yet collecting consistent information across Indonesia's vast archipelago remains a significant challenge.

Presentations highlighted different approaches to strengthening monitoring systems and generating the evidence needed for informed decision-making. For example, Nurul W.Q. Manik of Rekam Nusantara Foundation highlighted the role of technological innovation. Drawing from her experience of collaborative monitoring of Wedgefish and Giant Guitarfish on the North Coast of Central Java through the IKAN project, she showcased how digital platforms and collaborative monitoring can improve data collection efficiency, strengthen species traceability, and provide near real-time information to support management decisions. As fisheries monitoring increasingly embrace digital tools, initiatives such as IKAN demonstrate how technology can complement field-based conservation efforts.

Ayi Warmia of Rekam highlighted how fishers and coastal communities can play an active role in monitoring threatened species while participating in awareness-raising activities. Rather than treating communities as passive beneficiaries of conservation programs, the initiative places them at the centre of monitoring efforts and encourages local stewardship of marine resources.

Although different in approach, both initiatives share a common goal: improving the quality and availability of data for conservation and management. Whether through community participation or digital innovation, these efforts demonstrate how collaborative monitoring can help bridge critical information gaps and support more informed decision-making for sharks and rays in Indonesia.
The importance of working with Fishers and Coastal Communities

Across Indonesia, fishers are often the people most directly connected to sharks and rays. They encounter these species at sea, land them at ports, and play a central role in fisheries that support coastal livelihoods.

Fiki Hidayati of Rekam presented her experience of working with communities in Rembang, Central Java, which focused on approaches to involving fishers as active partners in shark and ray conservation initiatives. Building trust and open communication through long-term engagement with the community were critical to creating opportunities for fishers to contribute valuable information while also becoming advocates for sustainable practices.[CM1] [JM2]

Anhar Muslim of IPB University showed how shark landings data can be utilised within fisheries to better understand the different species entering local supply chains. The information provides an important foundation for understanding fisheries dynamics and identifying which species to prioritize for evidence-based management.

Nadia Maula, a PhD student at Liverpool John Moores University, supported by the UK Government’s International Wildlife Trade Programme, also presented at the conference. She reflected on her previous work of integrating local community perspectives for Rhinoray Conservation in Karimunjawa National Park, sharing practical lessons from community engagement approaches developed through the Elasmobranch Project Indonesia. She also contributed to the panel session “Net for Gains: Engaging Fishers to Protect Sharks and Rays,” led by Dr. Hollie Booth and alongside several fellow conservationists, during which Nadia explored both the importance of local participation in conservation and the challenges of implementing inclusive, context-specific approaches.

A key message emerging from the panel was that there is no single solution for effective conservation.  Instead, strategies must be tailored to the social and ecological context of each community and understanding fisheries and engaging communities are both essential components for successful sustainable management.
Understanding Trade from Markets to Consumers

Understanding trade dynamics remains a critical component of conservation and governance efforts in Indonesia. Several presentations explored different dimensions of trade, from consumer demand to supply chains and emerging online marketplaces.

One often-overlooked aspect of shark and ray conservation is the role of consumers. Addressing this gap, Wahyu Putri Fajar Rahmalinda of Rekam presented her research examining meat consumption patterns across Java, exploring the social and economic factors that shape demand. The findings challenged common assumptions about consumer motivations. As Rahmalinda explained, "Shark meat consumption in Java was shaped not by sustainability concerns, but by affordability and accessibility." By highlighting the factors that drive purchasing decisions, the study provides valuable insights for policymakers and conservation practitioners seeking to address demand within shark and ray supply chains.

Meanwhile, Dr Andhika Prasetyo of BRIN explored the growing role of digital marketplaces in the trade of shark and ray products. By documenting species and products being sold online, the study highlighted the need for monitoring systems that can adapt to rapidly changing trade practices and increasingly, digital supply chains.

Building on this understanding, Siti Zanuba Aisyah of Rekam presented some of the governance challenges and opportunities for strengthening oversight within domestic trade networks. The findings contribute to a clearer understanding of how products move through complex supply chains and where interventions may be most effective.

Together, these studies painted a broader picture of trade in Indonesia, illustrating how conservation challenges extend beyond fishing activities and into markets, consumer behaviour, and emerging online commerce.
Turning CITES Commitments into Action

As more shark and ray species become listed under CITES, countries face growing challenges in ensuring that international commitments translate into effective implementation at national and local levels. Several presentations explored how Indonesia is addressing these challenges through research, governance, and institutional development.

Nurmila Anwar of IPB University examined trade and distribution pathways for one of the world's most threatened ray species, providing valuable insights into how products move through trade networks and where monitoring and enforcement efforts may need to be strengthened.

Taking a broader perspective, Efin Muttaqin of Rekam highlighted that while CITES listings are often viewed as major conservation milestones, there is often complex work involved in driving progress, such as strengthening institutions, aligning policies, improving coordination among agencies, and ensuring that management measures can be implemented effectively on the ground. To ensure sustainability andlasting conservation outcomes, he noted, systems, partnerships, and resources are needed to turn those commitments into meaningful action.

This topic was further explored during an international expert panel discussion "Listed but Vulnerable: Enforcing CITES for Sharks and Rays. Moderated by Cefas' Dr Joanna Murray and Dr Heidi Retnoningtyas of Rekam, the session heard from conservation, science, and customs enforcement experts from Indonesia, Sri Lanka and South America to discuss policy interventions aimed at turning CITES listings into effective action.
Looking Ahead
Participation in Sharks International 2026 provided an opportunity not only to share findings from Indonesia, but also to learn from experiences across the world.

The presentations and discussions reflected the complexity of shark and ray conservation, where ecological sustainability is closely linked to fisheries, livelihoods, governance systems, trade networks, and community participation. From strengthening monitoring systems and embracing digital innovation to understanding trade dynamics and implementing international regulations, each contribution highlighted a different piece of a much larger conservation challenge.

At the same time, the conference reinforced the value of collaboration. The work presented by Rekam, Cefas, MMAF, IPB University, BRIN, and other partners demonstrate how conservation outcomes can be strengthened when researchers, government agencies, local communities, and international organizations work together towards shared goals.

As global attention on sharks and rays continues to grow, partnerships such as these will remain essential in ensuring that conservation efforts are informed by science, grounded in local realities, and capable of delivering meaningful and lasting impact.

The Illegal Wildlife Trade funded project, Strengthening Indonesia’s capacity to reduce illegal shark fisheries and trade, is led by Cefas with Indonesian partners, Directorate of Conservation and Marine Biodiversity, Ministry of Marine Affairs and Fisheries (MMAF).
